description: This test case evaluates the RAG system's robustness in retrieving a specific, rare, or hard-to-find fact (the 'needle') from a large context ('haystack').
question: Analyze how the annealing process in the back-end-of-line (BEOL) copper interconnect integration at the 3nm technology node contributes to resistance reduction without compromising reliability and yield. Discuss the physical mechanisms involved and how these effects are experimentally confirmed according to the provided context.

gold_context_sources.evidence: Post CuBS dep, annealing is performed in the Applied Materials Producer ® PYRA® system (Fig. 12). Annealing provides Cu grain size increase confirmed with High Resolution (HR)-TEM (Fig. 13) and Mn movement away from the bulk interconnect is confirmed by SIMS measurements (Fig 18). Both these effects provide low resistance interconnect [5]. The grain size and Rs (Fig. 14) correlate well with Mayadas-Shatzkes model (Fig. 15) which confirms reduction of grain-grain binding energy (Fig. 16, 17), resulting in grain boundary dissolution and larger grains. With the scaling of device dimensions [1], BEOL interconnect (Fig. 1) resistance, reliability and process yield challenges increase proportionally. There is an urgent need for process innovations which enable resistance reduction without compromising reliability and yield. We propose a novel integration sequence (Fig. 2) comprising of integrated selective barrier CuBS, followed by annealing and CMP.

ground_truth_answer: The annealing process in the BEOL copper interconnect integration at the 3nm technology node plays a crucial role in reducing interconnect resistance while maintaining reliability and yield. Physically, annealing increases the copper grain size, as confirmed by High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM). Larger grains reduce the number of grain boundaries, which are significant scattering centers that increase electrical resistance. Furthermore, Secondary Ion Mass Spectrometry (SIMS) measurements confirm the movement of manganese (Mn) away from the bulk interconnect, which likely helps in stabilizing the copper microstructure and reducing impurity-related scattering. The observed grain size and sheet resistance correlate well with the Mayadas-Shatzkes model, which relates grain boundary scattering to resistivity. This model's confirmation through experimental data shows that annealing effectively reduces the grain-grain binding energy, promoting grain boundary dissolution and leading to larger grains. This physical transformation results in a low resistance copper interconnect. The novel integration sequence combining integrated selective barrier copper barrier seed (CuBS), annealing, and chemical mechanical planarization (CMP) enables this resistance reduction without compromising reliability and process yield, which are increasingly challenging as device dimensions scale down. Thus, annealing serves as a critical process innovation to meet the stringent performance and reliability demands at advanced technology nodes.
